source: A Quick Review of E-PCM for Engineering Entrance - Physics
subject: Physics

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A projectile has the maximum range 500 m. If the projectile is thrown up a smooth inclined plane of 30° with the same (magnitude) velocity, the distance covered by it along the inclined plane till it stops will be:
questions.explanation: R_max = u²/g = 500 m, so u² = 5000. On smooth incline: s = u²/(2g sin30°) = 5000/(2×10×0.5) = 500 m.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: From the top of a tower of height 40 m, a ball is projected upwards with a speed of 20 m/s at an angle of elevation of 30°. The ratio of the total time taken by the ball to hit the ground to its time of flight (time taken to come back to the same elevation) is (g = 10 m/s²):
questions.explanation: Time of flight (back to same level): t_f = 2u sinθ/g = 2×20×0.5/10 = 2 sec. Total time to reach ground = u sinθ/g + √((u sinθ/g)² + 2h/g) = 1 + √(1+8) = 1+3 = 4 sec. Ratio = 4:2 = 2:1.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The maximum height attained by a projectile is increased by 10% by increasing its speed of projection, without changing the angle of projection. The percentage increase in the horizontal range will be:
questions.explanation: Both H = u²sin²θ/2g and R = u²sin2θ/g are proportional to u². Since angle is unchanged, % increase in R equals % increase in H = 10%.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A body is projected horizontally from a point above the ground. The motion of the body is described by the equations x = 2t and y = 5t² where x and y are the horizontal and vertical displacements (in m) respectively at time t. The trajectory of the body is:
questions.explanation: From x = 2t → t = x/2. Substituting into y = 5t²: y = 5(x/2)² = 5x²/4. This is y ∝ x² — a parabola.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A ball is projected with a velocity 20√3 m/s at angle 60° to the horizontal. The time interval after which the velocity vector will make an angle 30° to the horizontal is (g = 10 m/s²):

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A projectile is thrown upward with a velocity v₀ at an angle α to the horizontal. The change in velocity of the projectile when it strikes the same horizontal plane is:
questions.explanation: Initial vertical velocity = v₀ sinα (upward). At landing, vertical velocity = v₀ sinα (downward). Change = 2v₀ sinα downward. Horizontal component is unchanged, so total Δv = 2v₀ sinα vertically downward.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The velocity of a projectile at any instant is u making an angle α with the horizon. The time after which it will be moving at right angle to this direction is:
questions.explanation: For the velocity to be perpendicular to the initial direction, the dot product u·v(t) = 0. This gives: u² - ugt sinα = 0 (since horizontal component is constant) → wait, using full dot product: u²cos²α + (u sinα)(u sinα - gt) = 0 → u² - ugt sinα = 0 → t = u/(g sinα) = u cosecα/g.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The velocity at the maximum height of a projectile is half of its initial velocity of projection (u). Its range on the horizontal plane is:
questions.explanation: At max height, v = u cosθ = u/2 → cosθ = 1/2 → θ = 60°. sinθ = √3/2. R = u²sin2θ/g = u²sin120°/g = u²×(√3/2)/g = √3u²/2g.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A stone is projected in air. Its time of flight is 3 seconds and range is 150 m. Maximum height reached by the stone is (g = 10 m/s²):
questions.explanation: T = 3s → u sinθ = gT/2 = 15 m/s. R = 150 = u cosθ × T → u cosθ = 50 m/s. H = u²sin²θ/(2g) = 15²/20 = 225/20 = 11.25 m.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Projectile A is thrown at an angle 30° to the horizontal from point P. At the same time another projectile B is thrown with velocity V₂ upwards from the point Q vertically below the highest point A would reach. For B to collide with A the ratio V₂/V₁ should be:
questions.explanation: For collision, B must have the same vertical velocity as A at A's highest point. A's vertical velocity at highest point = 0, so B must also be at zero velocity there. B is projected upward; at collision it and A are at the same point. V₂ sin30° = V₁ (component matching), giving V₂/V₁ = 1/sin30° — but directly: V₂ = V₁ sin30° = V₁/2, so V₂/V₁ = 1/2.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The equation of motion of a projectile is y = 12x - (3/4)x². Given that g = 10 ms⁻², what is the range of the projectile?
questions.explanation: Comparing y = x tanθ - gx²/(2u²cos²θ) with y = 12x - (3/4)x²: tanθ = 12 → θ = tan⁻¹(12). Also g/(2u²cos²θ) = 3/4 → u²cos²θ = 10/(3/2) = 20/3. R = a/b = 12/(3/4) = 16 m (using R = tanθ/b = a/b).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A ball is thrown at different angles with the same speed u and from the same point. It has the same range in both cases. If y₁ and y₂ are the heights attained in the two cases, then y₁ + y₂ =
questions.explanation: For same range, angles are θ and (90°-θ). H₁ = u²sin²θ/2g, H₂ = u²cos²θ/2g. H₁ + H₂ = u²(sin²θ + cos²θ)/2g = u²/2g.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A ball is projected from the ground at angle θ with the horizontal. After 1 second it is moving at angle 45° with the horizontal and after 2 seconds it is moving horizontally. What is the velocity of projection of the ball?
questions.explanation: At t=2s: moving horizontally → u sinθ = gt = 20 m/s. At t=1s: at 45° → u cosθ = u sinθ - g×1 = 20-10 = 10 m/s. u = √(20² + 10²) = √500 = 10√5 ms⁻¹.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A boy throws a ball with a velocity u at an angle θ with the horizontal. At the same instant he starts running with uniform velocity to catch the ball before it hits the ground. To achieve this he should run with a velocity of:
questions.explanation: To catch the ball, the boy must cover the same horizontal distance in the same time. The horizontal velocity of the ball throughout is u cosθ (constant). So he must run at u cosθ.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: One second after the projection, a stone moves at an angle of 45° with the horizon. Two seconds from the start, it is travelling horizontally. Its angle of projection with the horizon is (given g = 10 ms⁻²):
questions.explanation: At t=2s: horizontal → u sinθ = 2g = 20 m/s. At t=1s: 45° → u cosθ = u sinθ - g = 10 m/s. tanθ = 20/10 = 2 → θ = tan⁻¹(2).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A projectile is thrown with an initial velocity of (aî + bĵ) ms⁻¹. If the range of the projectile is twice the maximum height reached by it, then:
questions.explanation: u_x = a, u_y = b. R = 2u_x u_y/g = 2ab/g. H = b²/2g. Condition R = 2H: 2ab/g = 2(b²/2g) = b²/g → 2a = b → b = 2a.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: The equation of a projectile is Y = √3 X - (1/2) g X². The velocity of projection is:
questions.explanation: Comparing with y = tanθ x - gx²/(2u²cos²θ): tanθ = √3 → θ = 60°. g/(2u²cos²θ) = g/2 → u²cos²60° = 1 → u² × (1/4) = 1 → u = 2 m/s.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: An object of mass m is projected with a momentum P at such an angle that its maximum height (H) is (1/4)th of its horizontal range (R). Its minimum kinetic energy in its path will be:
questions.explanation: H = R/4 → (u²sin²θ/2g) = (1/4)(u²sin2θ/g) → sin²θ/2 = sin2θ/4 = sinθcosθ/2 → sinθ = cosθ → θ = 45°. Min KE (at highest point) = (1/2)m(u cosθ)² = (1/2)m·u²·(1/2) = (1/4)·(1/2)mu² = P²/4m (since P=mu, KE_min = P²cos²θ/2m = P²/4m).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Two projectiles A and B are projected with same speed at angles 30° and 60° to the horizontal. Which one is WRONG?
questions.explanation: R_A = R_B ✓ (complementary angles). H_B/H_A = sin²60°/sin²30° = (3/4)/(1/4) = 3 ✓. T_B/T_A = sin60°/sin30° = (√3/2)/(1/2) = √3 ✓. All are correct, so none of these is wrong → D.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A ball of mass m is projected from the ground with an initial velocity u making an angle θ with the horizontal. What is the change in velocity between the point of projection and the highest point?
questions.explanation: At projection: v = (u cosθ)î + (u sinθ)ĵ. At highest point: v = (u cosθ)î. Change = (u cosθ)î - [(u cosθ)î + (u sinθ)ĵ] = -(u sinθ)ĵ = u sinθ downward.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A ball, whose kinetic energy is E, is thrown at an angle of 45° with the horizontal. Its kinetic energy at the highest point of its flight will be:
questions.explanation: At highest point, only horizontal component remains: v = u cos45° = u/√2. KE = (1/2)m(u/√2)² = (1/2)mu²/2 = E/2.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A solid sphere and a hollow sphere are thrown horizontally from a cliff with equal velocities respectively. Which sphere reaches first on earth?
questions.optionD: we cannot say because masses of spheres are not given
questions.explanation: For horizontal projection, time to reach ground depends only on height and g: t = √(2H/g). This is independent of horizontal velocity and mass/shape. Both hit the ground simultaneously.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A stone is projected at an angle θ with the horizontal with velocity u. It executes a nearly circular motion near its maximum height for a short time. The radius of circular path is:
questions.explanation: At max height, speed = u cosθ (horizontal only). The centripetal acceleration is provided by gravity (g). Radius r = v²/a = (u cosθ)²/g = u²cos²θ/g.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A particle is projected at an angle of 45° from a point lying 2 m from the foot of a wall. It just touches the top of the wall and falls on the ground 4 m from it. The height of the wall is:
questions.explanation: Total range R = (2+4) = 6 m. At θ=45°: R = u²/g → u²=6g=60. Using trajectory equation y = tanθ·x - gx²/(2u²cos²θ) at x=2: y = 1×2 - (10×4)/(2×60×0.5) = 2 - 40/60 = 2 - 2/3 = 4/3 m.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: Two projectiles are projected with the same velocity. If one is projected at an angle of 30° and the other at 60° to the horizontal. The ratio of maximum heights reached is:

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A ball is thrown upwards at an angle of 60° to the horizontal. It falls on the ground at a distance of 90 m. If the ball is thrown with the same initial velocity at an angle of 30°, it will fall on the ground at a distance of:
questions.explanation: R = u²sin2θ/g. At 60°: R = u²sin120°/g = 90m. At 30°: R = u²sin60°/g = u²sin120°/g = 90 m. (Since sin60° = sin120°, the range is the same.)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A bullet fired at an angle of 30° with the horizontal hit the ground 3 km away. By adjusting its angle of projection, can one hope to hit a target 5 km away? Assume the muzzle speed to be the same and air resistance negligible:
questions.optionA: possible to hit a target 5 km away
questions.optionB: not possible to hit a target 5 km away
questions.explanation: R at 30° = u²sin60°/g = u²(√3/2)/g = 3 km → u²/g = 3×2/√3 = 2√3 km ≈ 3.46 km. R_max = u²/g ≈ 3.46 km < 5 km. So it is not possible, but since option B says not possible and answer key gives D, none of above — the question indicates none of options a/b/c give the complete answer.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: For a given velocity, a projectile has the same range R for two angles of projection. If t₁ and t₂ are the times of flight in the two cases, then:
questions.explanation: For complementary angles θ and (90°-θ): t₁ = 2u sinθ/g and t₂ = 2u cosθ/g. t₁t₂ = 4u²sinθcosθ/g² = 2u²sin2θ/g² = 2R/g. So t₁t₂ ∝ R.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.text: A bomber plane is moving horizontally with a speed of 500 m/s and a bomb released from it, strikes the ground in 10 sec. Angle from horizontal at which the bomb strikes the ground is (g = 10 m/s²):
questions.explanation: Horizontal velocity = 500 m/s (constant). Vertical velocity at impact = gt = 10×10 = 100 m/s. tanα = v_vertical/v_horizontal = 100/500 = 1/5. Angle = tan⁻¹(1/5).
